revision.text:
{{特殊文字}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|[[ラサ]]のポタラ宮殿外の岩に刻まれた六字真言（左）。右は[[金剛薩埵]]の真言({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''六字大明呪'''（ろくじだいみょうじゅ）<ref name="Iwanami">『岩波 仏教辞典 第二版』 121-122頁。</ref>とは、[[仏教]]の[[陀羅尼]]（[[呪文]]）の1つ。[[サンスクリット]]の6つの[[音節]]からなる[[観世音菩薩]]の[[陀羅尼]]であり{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}、[[チベット語]]で六文字となることから'''六字真言'''（ろくじしんごん）ともいう{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|loc=[[今枝由郎]]・[[星泉]]「用語集」, p. 353}}。特に、[[チベット仏教]]圏の[[チベット]]や[[モンゴル]]の仏教徒が頻繁に唱える陀羅尼である<ref name="Iwanami"/>。ほかに六字と名のつく真言陀羅尼には、[[文殊菩薩]]の六字陀羅尼、『請観音経』を典拠とする六字章句陀羅尼がある{{Efn|坂内龍雄『真言陀羅尼』は、観音菩薩の六字真言に六字陀羅尼の呼称を与えている（同書 89頁）。}}。
==訳== この[[陀羅尼]]は'''オーン・マニパドメー・フーン'''{{Efn|ここでは[[アヌスヴァーラ|アヌナーシカ]]（仰月点）の表す鼻音（{{IAST|ṃ}} または {{Unicode|m̐}}）を「ン」と表記している。「オーム マ ニ パ ドメー フーム」{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}{{Efn|ここでは[[アヌスヴァーラ|アヌナーシカ]]の表す鼻音（{{IAST|ṃ}}）を {{IAST|m}} に置き換えている。}}、「オーム、マニ、パドメー、フーン」{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}、「オーム、マニパドメーフーン」{{Sfn|立川|1997|p=30}}とも表記される。}}（{{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}）の[[梵字]]六文字{{Efn|正確には、ここでの六は字数に拠るのではなく、音節 ({{IAST|akṣara}}) が6つあることを指している{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}。}}から構成される。
チベット仏教では'''オンマニペメフン'''{{Efn|チベット語のカナ転写の仕方は学者ごとに異なっており、統一されていない。ここでは{{Harvnb|今枝監訳|2015}}と{{Harvnb|田中|2009}}の表記に拠った。ほかに「オン・マニ・ペメ・フン」（チベット学者の[[石濱裕美子]]による{{Sfn|石濱編|2004|loc=石濱裕美子「観音菩薩に祝福された民」, p. 14}}）、「オムマニペメフム」（1910年代にチベットで僧院生活を経験した[[多田等観]]<ref>多田等観著、[[牧野文子]]編 『チベット滞在記』 〈講談社学術文庫〉、講談社、2009年、178頁。</ref>、文化人類学者・チベット学者の[[棚瀬慈郎]]<ref>棚瀬慈郎 『旅とチベットと僕』 講談社、2013年、80頁。</ref>、同じく村上大輔<ref>村上大輔 『チベット 聖地の路地裏 - 八年のラサ滞在記』 法蔵観、2016年、43頁。</ref>がこの表記を採用）、「オムマニペメフン」<ref name="Kawachen">ケルサン・タウワ編 『チベット語辞典 蔵日・日蔵』 カワチェン、2003年、蔵日218頁、日蔵29頁。</ref>、「オム・マニ・ペメ・フム」{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/オム・マニ・ペメ・フム/ ダライ・ラマ法王日本代表部事務所>オム・マニ・ペメ・フム]}}などの表記例がある。「オーム・マニペメ・フーム」{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|pp=78-79}}、「オーム・マニ・ペーメエ・フーム」<ref name="Iwanami"/>とも表記される。}}（{{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}{{Efn|発音: [om ma ni peː`me hum]<ref name="Kawachen"/>}}、漢訳: '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽'''）という。
*'''ॐ'''（oṃ、オーン） :短母音 '''अ'''（a）と 短母音 '''उ'''（u）が結合し、長母音 '''ओ'''（o）となったものと子音 '''म्'''（m）の組合せで '''ओम्'''（om）となり、さらに子音が鼻音化し、'''ओं'''（oṃ）となる。これが簡略化され、'''ॐ'''（oṃ）と表記される。'''ॐ''' は[[神聖]]さを象徴する[[音節]]として機能する。 {{see also|オーム (聖音)}} *'''मणि'''（maṇi、マニ） :「宝石」や「宝珠」を意味する。 *'''पद्मे'''（padme、パドメ） :「蓮の中の」または「蓮の上の」を意味する。「[[ハス|蓮]]」を意味する '''पद्म'''（padma、パドマ）が[[処格]]に[[格変化]]したもので、場所を示している。 *'''हूँ'''（hūṃ、フーン） :子音 '''ह'''（h）と、母音 '''उ'''（u）が長音化した '''ू'''（ū）と、子音 '''म'''（m）が鼻音化した '''ं'''（ṃ）の組合せで成り立っている。'''हूँ''' は[[成就]]を象徴する[[音節]]として機能する。
=== 語釈 === マニは「[[如意宝珠|宝珠]]」、パドメーは「[[蓮華]]」を意味する。この陀羅尼には様々な解釈がある。[[伊藤武_(インド研究者)|伊藤武]]によれば、「宝珠」は男性原理としての[[方便]]{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}、「蓮華」は女性原理としての[[般若]]{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}、フーンは呪文の完成を意味する。[[密教|金剛乗]]の瑜伽においては、この陀羅尼は男尊と女尊の結合に象徴される[[空 (仏教)|空性]]の[[悟り|覚り]]を示し{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|p=23}}、その場合の宝珠は[[金剛杵]]（ヴァジュラ）であらわされる男性器を暗示しているという解釈がある{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|p=50}}。
;呼格とする説 :[[ドナルド・ロペス]]によれば、マニパドメーは「マニパドマ」または女性形の「マニパドマー」の[[呼格]]であり、「宝の蓮華を持つ者よ」という意味になる。マニパドマは菩薩の名でもある。呼格とする説はほかに{{仮リンク|フレデリック・ウィリアム・トーマス|en|Frederick William Thomas (philologist)}}、{{仮リンク|アウグスト・ヘルマン・フランケ (チベット学者)|label=フランケ|en|August Hermann Francke (Tibetologist)}}、[[ステン・コノウ]]、{{仮リンク|デイヴィッド・スネルグローブ|label=スネルグローブ|en|David Snellgrove}}らが述べている{{Sfn|Lopez|1998|pages=114-134}}。 ;「マニ」を呼格・「パドメー」を処格とする説 :伊藤武は「マニパドメー」を「マニ」と「パドメー」とに分かち、前者を[[呼格]]、後者を[[処格]]と解して、「オーン、蓮華〔の中、の上〕におわします宝珠よ、フーン」と和訳している{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}。
==原典== 六字真言の最も古い典拠は[[大乗仏教]]の[[経典]]『カーランダヴューハ・スートラ』 ({{IAST|kāraṇḍavyūha-sūtra}}) である{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}。観音菩薩の説話を記した『カーランダヴューハ』は、[[ネパール]]とチベットで観音信仰の根本経典として重んじられる{{Sfn|田中|2009|pages=46-47}}。同経典ではこの[[真言#明呪|明呪]]はシャダクシャリー・マハーヴィディヤー（六字大明）と称されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}。
== チベット仏教 == 観音菩薩の六字真言はチベットの人々がしきりに唱えることで有名である{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}。チベット語ではイゲドゥクパ（{{ラテン翻字|bo|yi ge drug pa}}, 「六つの音節」の意{{Efn|{{ラテン翻字|bo|yi ge}}には{{IAST|akṣara}}と同様に音節の意味もある。}}）と称される。
チベットには、自国が昔から観音菩薩に教化され導かれてきた国であるという歴史観があり{{Sfn|石濱編|2004|loc=石濱裕美子「観音菩薩に祝福された民」, p. 14}}、建国の王[[ソンツェン・ガンポ]]や歴代[[ダライ・ラマ]]は観音菩薩の[[化身]]とされる{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|loc=[[今枝由郎]]・[[星泉]]「用語集」, p. 356}}。そのためチベットでは六字真言の信仰が盛んで、人々によく唱えられるほか、「[[マニ石]]」と呼ばれる岩や「[[マニ車]]」（マニコル）と呼ばれる法具にも刻まれている。
『カーランダヴューハ』とその所説である六字真言は、早くも古代王国時代（[[吐蕃]]）に伝わっていた（チベットの歴史観では前伝期にあたる）。そのことは[[敦煌文献]]のなかにチベット語の六字真言の記された写本のあることからも裏づけられる。9世紀に編纂されたとされるチベット語の一切経目録『デンカルマ目録』にも『カーランダヴューハ』が記載されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=200}}。後世には、[[テルマ (宗教)|テルマ]]に分類される史書『マニカンブム』 ({{ラテン翻字|bo|ma ṇi bka' 'bum}}) などで六字真言の複雑な教義が展開された<ref name="Iwanami"/>。
=== ダライ・ラマ14世による説明 === [[ダライ・ラマ14世]]によると、オム・マニ・ペメ・フムは、細かく分けるとオム・マ・ニ・ペ・メ・フムという六つの真言（シックス・[[音節|シラブル]]・マントラ）で構成されている。ダライ・ラマは、「これら六つの真言は、私たちの不浄な身体・言葉・思考を、完全に統一された秩序と知恵の教えの道に導くことにより、仏陀になれる」の意と説明する。オムは身体・言葉・思考を表している。マニが宝石を意味し、秩序、慈悲、他者への思いやりなど悟りを開くための要素。ペメが蓮を意味し、矛盾から救い出す知恵の本質を示す。フムが、分離できないものを意味し、秩序と知恵の調和による純粋な境地を表している{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"}}。
=== 六字真言と六道 === {{出典の明記|section=n|date=2016-11}} チベットでは、六字を[[六道]]の各道に充て、一語一語にそれぞれの罪を浄化する意味を持たせている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|pp=200-201}}。この教義は14世紀に著された史書『王統明鏡史』第4章にも記されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|pp=200-201}}{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|p=65}}。
{| border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="5" style="white-space: nowrap" |- ! 梵字 !!蔵文!!{{Fontsize|77%|ローマ字}}!!浄化の対象 !! 六道 |- !ॐ |ཨོཾ |oṃ | 自我・高慢（慢） || 天 |- !म |མ |ma | 嫉妬・娯楽への渇望（悪見） || 修羅 |- !णि |ཎི |ṇi | 欲望・欲求（無明） || 人間 |- !प |པ |pa | 無知・偏見（痴） || 畜生 |- !द्मे |དྨེ |dme | 貧窮・所有欲（貪） || 餓鬼 |- !हूं |ཧཱུྃ |hūṃ | 憤怒・憎悪（瞋） || 地獄 |- |}
== 漢伝仏教 == 六字真言の典拠である『カーランダヴューハ』は[[北宋]]でインド出身の訳経僧、天息災によって『仏説大乗荘厳宝王経』として漢訳された。この真言は同経典では「六字大明陀羅尼」と称され、「'''唵'''<sup>引</sup> '''麼''' '''抳''' '''鉢''' '''訥銘'''<sup>二合</sup><sub>引</sub> '''吽'''<sup>引</sup>」（おんまにぱどめいうん）と音訳されている。この経典は中国・日本ではあまり普及しなかった{{Sfn|田中|2009|pages=46-47}}。
== 功徳 == 『カーランダ・ヴューハ・スートラ』によると、六字真言を祈念する者は輪廻することなく[[観音菩薩|観自在菩薩]]の広大な宇宙を見ることができ、この真言を唱えると[[ダイヤモンド|金剛]]の身体を得て、弁才を獲得し、悟りへ到ることができるという功徳が説かれている<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author = 佐久間留理子 |url = https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ibk/64/1/64_KJ00010199565/_pdf/-char/ja |title = 『カーランダ・ヴューハ・スートラ』における六字真言と准胝陀羅尼 |date = 2015-12 |journal = 印度學佛敏學研究第64巻第1号}}</ref>。
『仏説大乗荘厳宝王経』では、この陀羅尼を唱えた際の効果が説かれている。それによると、この陀羅尼を唱えれば様々な災害や病気、盗賊などから観世音菩薩が護ってくれるという。
古代[[チベット]]王[[ソンツェン・ガンポ]]の遺書とされる[[テルマ (宗教)|埋蔵経]]『マニ・カンブン』には、六字真言よって罪障が滅せられ、清浄となって[[極楽|極楽浄土]]に再生することが説かれている<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author = 槇殿伴子 |url = https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ibk/69/2/69_810/_pdf |title = 『マニ・カンブン』における如来蔵思想 |date = 2021-3 |journal = 印度學佛敎學硏究第69巻第2号 }}</ref>。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===注釈=== {{Notelist}} ===出典=== {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * ''The Origins of Om Manipadme Hum''（アレクサンダー・スタッドホルム著、ニューヨーク州立大学出版局、2002年） *{{Cite book|洋書|first=Donald |last=Lopez |year=1998 |title=''Prisoners of Shangri-La: Tibetan Buddhism and the West |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=0-226-49311-3 |ref={{SfnRef|Lopez|1998}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=フィリップ・ローソン |others=[[森雅秀]]・森喜子訳 |year=1994 |title=イメージの博物誌25 聖なるチベット |publisher=平凡社 |ref={{SfnRef|ローソン著, 森訳|1994}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=立川武蔵|authorlink=立川武蔵 |year=1997 |title=マンダラ瞑想法 - 密教のフィールドワーク |series=角川選書 |publisher=角川書店 |ref={{SfnRef|立川|1997}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=森雅秀|authorlink=森雅秀 |year=2001 |title=インド密教の仏たち |publisher=春秋社 |ref={{SfnRef|森|2001}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=石濱裕美子編著|authorlink=石濱裕美子 |year=2004 |title=エリア・スタディーズ 38 チベットを知るための50章 |publisher=明石書店 |ref={{SfnRef|石濱編|2004}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=田中公明|authorlink=田中公明 |year=2009 |title=チベットの仏たち |publisher=方丈堂出版 |ref={{SfnRef|田中|2009}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤武 |year=2011 |title=図説 ヨーガ大全 |publisher=佼成出版社 |ref={{SfnRef|伊藤|2011}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=ソナム・ギェルツェン |others=[[今枝由郎]]監訳 |year=2015 |title=チベット仏教王伝 - ソンツェン・ガンポ物語 |series=岩波文庫 |publisher=岩波書店 |ref={{SfnRef|今枝監訳|2015}}}}（『王統明鏡史』の第17章までの和訳） *{{Cite book|和書|author=佐久間留理子 |year=2015 |title=観音菩薩 - 変幻自在な姿をとる救済者 |publisher=春秋社 |ref={{SfnRef|佐久間|2015}}}}
==関連項目== *[[マントラ]] *[[西遊記]] - [[釈迦如来]]が[[孫悟空]]を[[五行山]]に封印する際、仕上げに六字大明呪の書かれた[[符]]を使用している。 *[[太祖王建]] (韓国ドラマ) - [[後高句麗]]の王になった怪僧・弓裔（クンイェ）が唱えることを強制している。 *[[w:sa:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ|ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]]{{sa icon}} *[[w:bo:ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།|ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།]]{{bo icon}} *[[w:zh:六字真言|六字真言]]{{zh icon}}
== 外部リンク == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Om Mani Padme Hum をテーマにした曲(Tibetan) ]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Ja import oversized]] to [[Category:jawiki oversized to be reviewed]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
<references group="lower-alpha"/>
{{citation needed|Om mani padme hum}}

{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[六字大明呪]] to [[Om mani padme hum]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{特殊文字}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|[[ラサ]]のポタラ宮殿外の岩に刻まれた六字真言（左）。右は[[金剛薩埵]]の真言({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''六字大明呪'''（ろくじだいみょうじゅ）<ref name="Iwanami">『岩波 仏教辞典 第二版』 121-122頁。</ref>とは、[[仏教]]の[[陀羅尼]]（[[呪文]]）の1つ。[[サンスクリット]]の6つの[[音節]]からなる[[観世音菩薩]]の[[陀羅尼]]であり{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}、[[チベット語]]で六文字となることから'''六字真言'''（ろくじしんごん）ともいう{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|loc=[[今枝由郎]]・[[星泉]]「用語集」, p. 353}}。特に、[[チベット仏教]]圏の[[チベット]]や[[モンゴル]]の仏教徒が頻繁に唱える陀羅尼である<ref name="Iwanami"/>。ほかに六字と名のつく真言陀羅尼には、[[文殊菩薩]]の六字陀羅尼、『請観音経』を典拠とする六字章句陀羅尼がある{{Efn|坂内龍雄『真言陀羅尼』は、観音菩薩の六字真言に六字陀羅尼の呼称を与えている（同書 89頁）。}}。
==訳== この[[陀羅尼]]は'''オーン・マニパドメー・フーン'''{{Efn|ここでは[[アヌスヴァーラ|アヌナーシカ]]（仰月点）の表す鼻音（{{IAST|ṃ}} または {{Unicode|m̐}}）を「ン」と表記している。「オーム マ ニ パ ドメー フーム」{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}{{Efn|ここでは[[アヌスヴァーラ|アヌナーシカ]]の表す鼻音（{{IAST|ṃ}}）を {{IAST|m}} に置き換えている。}}、「オーム、マニ、パドメー、フーン」{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}、「オーム、マニパドメーフーン」{{Sfn|立川|1997|p=30}}とも表記される。}}（{{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}）の[[梵字]]六文字{{Efn|正確には、ここでの六は字数に拠るのではなく、音節 ({{IAST|akṣara}}) が6つあることを指している{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}。}}から構成される。
チベット仏教では'''オンマニペメフン'''{{Efn|チベット語のカナ転写の仕方は学者ごとに異なっており、統一されていない。ここでは{{Harvnb|今枝監訳|2015}}と{{Harvnb|田中|2009}}の表記に拠った。ほかに「オン・マニ・ペメ・フン」（チベット学者の[[石濱裕美子]]による{{Sfn|石濱編|2004|loc=石濱裕美子「観音菩薩に祝福された民」, p. 14}}）、「オムマニペメフム」（1910年代にチベットで僧院生活を経験した[[多田等観]]<ref>多田等観著、[[牧野文子]]編 『チベット滞在記』 〈講談社学術文庫〉、講談社、2009年、178頁。</ref>、文化人類学者・チベット学者の[[棚瀬慈郎]]<ref>棚瀬慈郎 『旅とチベットと僕』 講談社、2013年、80頁。</ref>、同じく村上大輔<ref>村上大輔 『チベット 聖地の路地裏 - 八年のラサ滞在記』 法蔵観、2016年、43頁。</ref>がこの表記を採用）、「オムマニペメフン」<ref name="Kawachen">ケルサン・タウワ編 『チベット語辞典 蔵日・日蔵』 カワチェン、2003年、蔵日218頁、日蔵29頁。</ref>、「オム・マニ・ペメ・フム」{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/オム・マニ・ペメ・フム/ ダライ・ラマ法王日本代表部事務所>オム・マニ・ペメ・フム]}}などの表記例がある。「オーム・マニペメ・フーム」{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|pp=78-79}}、「オーム・マニ・ペーメエ・フーム」<ref name="Iwanami"/>とも表記される。}}（{{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}{{Efn|発音: [om ma ni peː`me hum]<ref name="Kawachen"/>}}、漢訳: '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽'''）という。
*'''ॐ'''（oṃ、オーン） :短母音 '''अ'''（a）と 短母音 '''उ'''（u）が結合し、長母音 '''ओ'''（o）となったものと子音 '''म्'''（m）の組合せで '''ओम्'''（om）となり、さらに子音が鼻音化し、'''ओं'''（oṃ）となる。これが簡略化され、'''ॐ'''（oṃ）と表記される。'''ॐ''' は[[神聖]]さを象徴する[[音節]]として機能する。 {{see also|オーム (聖音)}} *'''मणि'''（maṇi、マニ） :「宝石」や「宝珠」を意味する。 *'''पद्मे'''（padme、パドメ） :「蓮の中の」または「蓮の上の」を意味する。「[[ハス|蓮]]」を意味する '''पद्म'''（padma、パドマ）が[[処格]]に[[格変化]]したもので、場所を示している。 *'''हूँ'''（hūṃ、フーン） :子音 '''ह'''（h）と、母音 '''उ'''（u）が長音化した '''ू'''（ū）と、子音 '''म'''（m）が鼻音化した '''ं'''（ṃ）の組合せで成り立っている。'''हूँ''' は[[成就]]を象徴する[[音節]]として機能する。
=== 語釈 === マニは「[[如意宝珠|宝珠]]」、パドメーは「[[蓮華]]」を意味する。この陀羅尼には様々な解釈がある。[[伊藤武_(インド研究者)|伊藤武]]によれば、「宝珠」は男性原理としての[[方便]]{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}、「蓮華」は女性原理としての[[般若]]{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}、フーンは呪文の完成を意味する。[[密教|金剛乗]]の瑜伽においては、この陀羅尼は男尊と女尊の結合に象徴される[[空 (仏教)|空性]]の[[悟り|覚り]]を示し{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|p=23}}、その場合の宝珠は[[金剛杵]]（ヴァジュラ）であらわされる男性器を暗示しているという解釈がある{{Sfn|ローソン著, 森訳|1994|p=50}}。
;呼格とする説 :[[ドナルド・ロペス]]によれば、マニパドメーは「マニパドマ」または女性形の「マニパドマー」の[[呼格]]であり、「宝の蓮華を持つ者よ」という意味になる。マニパドマは菩薩の名でもある。呼格とする説はほかに{{仮リンク|フレデリック・ウィリアム・トーマス|en|Frederick William Thomas (philologist)}}、{{仮リンク|アウグスト・ヘルマン・フランケ (チベット学者)|label=フランケ|en|August Hermann Francke (Tibetologist)}}、[[ステン・コノウ]]、{{仮リンク|デイヴィッド・スネルグローブ|label=スネルグローブ|en|David Snellgrove}}らが述べている{{Sfn|Lopez|1998|pages=114-134}}。 ;「マニ」を呼格・「パドメー」を処格とする説 :伊藤武は「マニパドメー」を「マニ」と「パドメー」とに分かち、前者を[[呼格]]、後者を[[処格]]と解して、「オーン、蓮華〔の中、の上〕におわします宝珠よ、フーン」と和訳している{{Sfn|伊藤|2011|pp=236-237}}。
==原典== 六字真言の最も古い典拠は[[大乗仏教]]の[[経典]]『カーランダヴューハ・スートラ』 ({{IAST|kāraṇḍavyūha-sūtra}}) である{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}。観音菩薩の説話を記した『カーランダヴューハ』は、[[ネパール]]とチベットで観音信仰の根本経典として重んじられる{{Sfn|田中|2009|pages=46-47}}。同経典ではこの[[真言#明呪|明呪]]はシャダクシャリー・マハーヴィディヤー（六字大明）と称されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=193}}。
== チベット仏教 == 観音菩薩の六字真言はチベットの人々がしきりに唱えることで有名である{{Sfn|森|2011|p=188}}。チベット語ではイゲドゥクパ（{{ラテン翻字|bo|yi ge drug pa}}, 「六つの音節」の意{{Efn|{{ラテン翻字|bo|yi ge}}には{{IAST|akṣara}}と同様に音節の意味もある。}}）と称される。
チベットには、自国が昔から観音菩薩に教化され導かれてきた国であるという歴史観があり{{Sfn|石濱編|2004|loc=石濱裕美子「観音菩薩に祝福された民」, p. 14}}、建国の王[[ソンツェン・ガンポ]]や歴代[[ダライ・ラマ]]は観音菩薩の[[化身]]とされる{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|loc=[[今枝由郎]]・[[星泉]]「用語集」, p. 356}}。そのためチベットでは六字真言の信仰が盛んで、人々によく唱えられるほか、「[[マニ石]]」と呼ばれる岩や「[[マニ車]]」（マニコル）と呼ばれる法具にも刻まれている。
『カーランダヴューハ』とその所説である六字真言は、早くも古代王国時代（[[吐蕃]]）に伝わっていた（チベットの歴史観では前伝期にあたる）。そのことは[[敦煌文献]]のなかにチベット語の六字真言の記された写本のあることからも裏づけられる。9世紀に編纂されたとされるチベット語の一切経目録『デンカルマ目録』にも『カーランダヴューハ』が記載されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|p=200}}。後世には、[[テルマ (宗教)|テルマ]]に分類される史書『マニカンブム』 ({{ラテン翻字|bo|ma ṇi bka' 'bum}}) などで六字真言の複雑な教義が展開された<ref name="Iwanami"/>。
=== ダライ・ラマ14世による説明 === [[ダライ・ラマ14世]]によると、オム・マニ・ペメ・フムは、細かく分けるとオム・マ・ニ・ペ・メ・フムという六つの真言（シックス・[[音節|シラブル]]・マントラ）で構成されている。ダライ・ラマは、「これら六つの真言は、私たちの不浄な身体・言葉・思考を、完全に統一された秩序と知恵の教えの道に導くことにより、仏陀になれる」の意と説明する。オムは身体・言葉・思考を表している。マニが宝石を意味し、秩序、慈悲、他者への思いやりなど悟りを開くための要素。ペメが蓮を意味し、矛盾から救い出す知恵の本質を示す。フムが、分離できないものを意味し、秩序と知恵の調和による純粋な境地を表している{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"}}。
=== 六字真言と六道 === {{出典の明記|section=n|date=2016-11}} チベットでは、六字を[[六道]]の各道に充て、一語一語にそれぞれの罪を浄化する意味を持たせている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|pp=200-201}}。この教義は14世紀に著された史書『王統明鏡史』第4章にも記されている{{Sfn|佐久間|2015|pp=200-201}}{{Sfn|今枝監訳|2015|p=65}}。
{| border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="5" style="white-space: nowrap" |- ! 梵字 !!蔵文!!{{Fontsize|77%|ローマ字}}!!浄化の対象 !! 六道 |- !ॐ |ཨོཾ |oṃ | 自我・高慢（慢） || 天 |- !म |མ |ma | 嫉妬・娯楽への渇望（悪見） || 修羅 |- !णि |ཎི |ṇi | 欲望・欲求（無明） || 人間 |- !प |པ |pa | 無知・偏見（痴） || 畜生 |- !द्मे |དྨེ |dme | 貧窮・所有欲（貪） || 餓鬼 |- !हूं |ཧཱུྃ |hūṃ | 憤怒・憎悪（瞋） || 地獄 |- |}
== 漢伝仏教 == 六字真言の典拠である『カーランダヴューハ』は[[北宋]]でインド出身の訳経僧、天息災によって『仏説大乗荘厳宝王経』として漢訳された。この真言は同経典では「六字大明陀羅尼」と称され、「'''唵'''<sup>引</sup> '''麼''' '''抳''' '''鉢''' '''訥銘'''<sup>二合</sup><sub>引</sub> '''吽'''<sup>引</sup>」（おんまにぱどめいうん）と音訳されている。この経典は中国・日本ではあまり普及しなかった{{Sfn|田中|2009|pages=46-47}}。
== 功徳 == 『カーランダ・ヴューハ・スートラ』によると、六字真言を祈念する者は輪廻することなく[[観音菩薩|観自在菩薩]]の広大な宇宙を見ることができ、この真言を唱えると[[ダイヤモンド|金剛]]の身体を得て、弁才を獲得し、悟りへ到ることができるという功徳が説かれている<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author = 佐久間留理子 |url = https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ibk/64/1/64_KJ00010199565/_pdf/-char/ja |title = 『カーランダ・ヴューハ・スートラ』における六字真言と准胝陀羅尼 |date = 2015-12 |journal = 印度學佛敏學研究第64巻第1号}}</ref>。
『仏説大乗荘厳宝王経』では、この陀羅尼を唱えた際の効果が説かれている。それによると、この陀羅尼を唱えれば様々な災害や病気、盗賊などから観世音菩薩が護ってくれるという。
古代[[チベット]]王[[ソンツェン・ガンポ]]の遺書とされる[[テルマ (宗教)|埋蔵経]]『マニ・カンブン』には、六字真言よって罪障が滅せられ、清浄となって[[極楽|極楽浄土]]に再生することが説かれている<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author = 槇殿伴子 |url = https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ibk/69/2/69_810/_pdf |title = 『マニ・カンブン』における如来蔵思想 |date = 2021-3 |journal = 印度學佛敎學硏究第69巻第2号 }}</ref>。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===注釈=== {{Notelist}} ===出典=== {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * ''The Origins of Om Manipadme Hum''（アレクサンダー・スタッドホルム著、ニューヨーク州立大学出版局、2002年） *{{Cite book|洋書|first=Donald |last=Lopez |year=1998 |title=''Prisoners of Shangri-La: Tibetan Buddhism and the West |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=0-226-49311-3 |ref={{SfnRef|Lopez|1998}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=フィリップ・ローソン |others=[[森雅秀]]・森喜子訳 |year=1994 |title=イメージの博物誌25 聖なるチベット |publisher=平凡社 |ref={{SfnRef|ローソン著, 森訳|1994}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=立川武蔵|authorlink=立川武蔵 |year=1997 |title=マンダラ瞑想法 - 密教のフィールドワーク |series=角川選書 |publisher=角川書店 |ref={{SfnRef|立川|1997}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=森雅秀|authorlink=森雅秀 |year=2001 |title=インド密教の仏たち |publisher=春秋社 |ref={{SfnRef|森|2001}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=石濱裕美子編著|authorlink=石濱裕美子 |year=2004 |title=エリア・スタディーズ 38 チベットを知るための50章 |publisher=明石書店 |ref={{SfnRef|石濱編|2004}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=田中公明|authorlink=田中公明 |year=2009 |title=チベットの仏たち |publisher=方丈堂出版 |ref={{SfnRef|田中|2009}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤武 |year=2011 |title=図説 ヨーガ大全 |publisher=佼成出版社 |ref={{SfnRef|伊藤|2011}}}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=ソナム・ギェルツェン |others=[[今枝由郎]]監訳 |year=2015 |title=チベット仏教王伝 - ソンツェン・ガンポ物語 |series=岩波文庫 |publisher=岩波書店 |ref={{SfnRef|今枝監訳|2015}}}}（『王統明鏡史』の第17章までの和訳） *{{Cite book|和書|author=佐久間留理子 |year=2015 |title=観音菩薩 - 変幻自在な姿をとる救済者 |publisher=春秋社 |ref={{SfnRef|佐久間|2015}}}}
==関連項目== *[[マントラ]] *[[西遊記]] - [[釈迦如来]]が[[孫悟空]]を[[五行山]]に封印する際、仕上げに六字大明呪の書かれた[[符]]を使用している。 *[[太祖王建]] (韓国ドラマ) - [[後高句麗]]の王になった怪僧・弓裔（クンイェ）が唱えることを強制している。 *[[w:sa:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ|ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]]{{sa icon}} *[[w:bo:ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།|ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།]]{{bo icon}} *[[w:zh:六字真言|六字真言]]{{zh icon}}
== 外部リンク == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Om Mani Padme Hum をテーマにした曲(Tibetan) ]
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{Buddhism2}}
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ろくしたいみようしゆ}} [[Category:観音菩薩]] [[Category:チベット仏教]] [[Category:陀羅尼]]

revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

{{citation needed|Om mani padme hum}}

revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=draft}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵|12=draft}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=draft}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼|12=draft}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=draft}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節|12=draft}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット|12=draft}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語|12=draft}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉|12=draft}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教|12=draft}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット|12=draft}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=draft}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩|12=draft}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字|12=draft}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=draft}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=draft}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=draft}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=draft}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便|12=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若|12=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗|12=draft}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵|12=draft}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ|12=draft}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=draft}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車|12=draft}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃|12=draft}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献|12=draft}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世|12=draft}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=draft}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記|12=draft}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=draft}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵|qq=draft}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]

Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字|qq=draft}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗|qq=draft}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵|qq=draft}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ|qq=draft}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車|qq=draft}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃|qq=draft}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献|qq=draft}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世|qq=draft}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記|qq=draft}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
<references group="lower-alpha"/>
{{citation needed|Om mani padme hum}}

revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵|qq=draft}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼|qq=draft}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節|qq=draft}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語|qq=draft}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉|qq=draft}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教|qq=draft}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字|qq=draft}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗|qq=draft}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵|qq=draft}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ|qq=draft}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車|qq=draft}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃|qq=draft}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献|qq=draft}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世|qq=draft}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記|qq=draft}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manjushri|qq=}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.

revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|ja|金剛薩埵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajrasattva|qq=}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dhāraṇī|ja|陀羅尼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dhāraṇī|qq=}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhism|qq=}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|ja|音節|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syllable|qq=}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|qq=}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Tibetan language|ja|チベット語|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tibetan language|qq=}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yutaka Imaeda|ja|今枝由郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutaka Imaeda|qq=}} and {{ill|Hoshii Izumi|ja|星泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshii Izumi|qq=}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|ja|チベット仏教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tibetan Buddhism|qq=}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|ja|チベット|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tibet|qq=}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manjushri|qq=}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|ja|梵字|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siddhaṃ script|qq=}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|ja|方便|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Upaya|qq=}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā|ja|般若|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prajñā|qq=}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Vajrayana|ja|密教|金剛乗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajrayana|qq=}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|ja|金剛杵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajra|qq=}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|ja|ソンツェン・ガンポ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Songtsen Gampo|qq=}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lamas|ja|ダライ・ラマ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dalai Lamas|qq=}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=}} and {{ill|Prayer wheels|ja|マニ車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer wheels|qq=}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tubo|ja|吐蕃|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tubo|qq=}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|ja|敦煌文献|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dunhuang manuscripts|qq=}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|ja|ダライ・ラマ14世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th Dalai Lama|qq=}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|ja|マントラ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mantra|qq=}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|ja|西遊記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Journey to the West|qq=}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|ja|孫悟空|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Wukong|qq=}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
<references group="lower-alpha"/>

On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=}}.

Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

revision.comment: Bot: update category → Dharani
revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
