revision.text:
{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

{{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

revision.comment: Bot: add wikidata link templates
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bar:Om mani padme hum]][[bg:Ом мани падме хум]][[bn:ওঁ মণিপদ্মে হূঁ]][[bo:ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།]][[bxr:Маани]][[ca:Om mani padme hum]][[commons:Om mani padme hum]][[cs:Óm mani padmé húm]][[da:Om mani padme hum]][[de:Om mani padme hum]][[en:Om mani padme hum]][[eo:Om mani padme hum]][[es:Om mani padme hum]][[et:Om mani padme hum]][[eu:Om mani padme hum]][[fi:Om mani padme hum]][[fr:Om mani padme hum]][[he:אום מאני פאדמה הום]][[hi:ओं मणिपद्मे हूं]][[hu:Om mani padme hum]][[id:Om mani padme hum]][[it:Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ]][[ja:六字大明呪]][[ka:ომ მანი პადმე ჰუმი]][[km:ឱំ មន្នី បទមេហុំ]][[ko:옴 마니 파드메 훔]][[lt:Om mani padme hum]][[ml:ഓം മണി പദ്മേ ഹൂം]][[mr:ॐ मणिपद्मे हूं]][[ne:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]][[nl:Om mani padme hum]][[no:Oṃ maṇi padme hūṃ]][[pa:ਓਮ ਮਣੀ ਪਦਮੇ ਹੂੰ]][[pl:Om mani padme hum]][[pt:Om mani padme hum]][[ru:Ом мани падме хум]][[sa:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]][[sk:Óm mani padmé húm]][[sl:Om mani padme hum]][[ta:ஓம் மணி பத்மே ஹூம்]][[th:โอม มณี ปัทเม หูม]][[tyv:Ом Мани Падме Хуң (дагда бижик)]][[uk:Ом мані падме хум]][[vi:Om Mani Padme Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh_yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bar:Om mani padme hum]][[bg:Ом мани падме хум]][[bn:ওঁ মণিপদ্মে হূঁ]][[bo:ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པ་དྨེ་ཧཱུྃ།]][[bxr:Маани]][[ca:Om mani padme hum]][[sn:commons:Om mani padme hum]][[cs:Óm mani padmé húm]][[da:Om mani padme hum]][[de:Om mani padme hum]][[en:Om mani padme hum]][[eo:Om mani padme hum]][[es:Om mani padme hum]][[et:Om mani padme hum]][[eu:Om mani padme hum]][[fi:Om mani padme hum]][[fr:Om mani padme hum]][[he:אום מאני פאדמה הום]][[hi:ओं मणिपद्मे हूं]][[hu:Om mani padme hum]][[id:Om mani padme hum]][[it:Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ]][[ja:六字大明呪]][[ka:ომ მანი პადმე ჰუმი]][[km:ឱំ មន្នី បទមេហុំ]][[ko:옴 마니 파드메 훔]][[lt:Om mani padme hum]][[ml:ഓം മണി പദ്മേ ഹൂം]][[mr:ॐ मणिपद्मे हूं]][[ne:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]][[nl:Om mani padme hum]][[no:Oṃ maṇi padme hūṃ]][[pa:ਓਮ ਮਣੀ ਪਦਮੇ ਹੂੰ]][[pl:Om mani padme hum]][[pt:Om mani padme hum]][[ru:Ом мани падме хум]][[sa:ॐ मणि पद्मे हूँ]][[sk:Óm mani padmé húm]][[sl:Om mani padme hum]][[ta:ஓம் மணி பத்மே ஹூம்]][[th:โอม มณี ปัทเม หูม]][[tyv:Ом Мани Падме Хуң (дагда бижик)]][[uk:Ом мані падме хум]][[vi:Om Mani Padme Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh_yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|qq=|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|qq=|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|qq=|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|qq=|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|qq=|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
