talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

हूं]][[hu:Om mani padme hum]][[id:Om mani padme hum]][[it:Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ]][[ja:六字大明呪]][[ka:ომ მანი პადმე ჰუმი]][[km:ឱំ មន្នី បទមេហុំ]][[ko:옴 마니 파드메 훔]][[lt:Om mani padme hum]][[ml:ഓം മണി പദ്മേ Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of [[:en:Vajrasattva|Vajrasattva]] ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the [[:en:Dharani|Dhāraṇī]] (mantras) in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six [[:en:Syllable|Syllable]]s in [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in [[:en:Lhasa Tibetan|Tibetan language]], it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc=[[:en:Yoshiro Imaeda|Yutaka Imaeda]] and [[:en:Izumi Hoshi|Hoshii Izumi]], "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in [[:en:Tibetan Buddhism|Tibetan Buddhism]], where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in [[:en:Tibet|Tibet]] and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the [[:en:Manjushri|Manjushri]] Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six [[:en:Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ script]] characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of [[:en:Upaya|Upaya]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of [[:en:Prajñā (Buddhism)|Prajñā]]{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the [[:en:Mikkyō|Vajrayana]] tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the [[:en:Vajra|Vajra]], an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as [[:en:Songtsen Gampo|Songtsen Gampo]] and the [[:en:Dalai Lama|Dalai Lamas]], are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Prayer wheel|Prayer wheels]].
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the [[:en:Tibetan Empire|Tubo]] period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the [[:en:Dunhuang manuscripts|Dunhuang manuscripts]]. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The [[:en:14th Dalai Lama|14th Dalai Lama]] explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * [[:en:Mantra|Mantra]] * [[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]] - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal [[:en:Sun Wukong|Sun Wukong]] under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

Hum]][[wuu:六字真言]][[zh-yue:六字大明咒]][[zh:六字真言]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q241652]] Wikidata name: om mani padme hum [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Vajrasattva}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dharani|en|Dharani|WD=Q1207590|lt=Dhāraṇī}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|en|Syllable|WD=Q8188|lt=Syllable}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Lhasa Tibetan|en|Lhasa Tibetan|WD=Q34271|lt=Tibetan language}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yoshiro Imaeda|en|Yoshiro Imaeda|WD=Q6099999|lt=Yutaka Imaeda}} and {{ill|Izumi Hoshi|en|Izumi Hoshi|WD=Q11512946|lt=Hoshii Izumi}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|WD=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|WD=Q17252|lt=Tibet}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|WD=Q471696|lt=Manjushri}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|WD=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=Upaya}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā (Buddhism)|en|Prajñā (Buddhism)|WD=Q849649|lt=Prajñā}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Vajrayana}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|WD=Q695709|lt=Vajra}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|en|Songtsen Gampo|WD=Q320122|lt=Songtsen Gampo}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lama|en|Dalai Lama|WD=Q37349|lt=Dalai Lamas}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Prayer wheel|en|Prayer wheel|WD=Q80876|lt=Prayer wheels}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tibetan Empire|en|Tibetan Empire|WD=Q2431480|lt=Tubo}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|en|Dunhuang manuscripts|WD=Q276864|lt=Dunhuang manuscripts}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|en|14th Dalai Lama|WD=Q17293|lt=14th Dalai Lama}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|WD=Q131510|lt=Mantra}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|en|Journey to the West|WD=Q70784|lt=Journey to the West}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|en|Sun Wukong|WD=Q11773777|lt=Sun Wukong}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]

June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters}} [[Image:Om_Mani_Padme_Hum.jpg|right|thumb|200px|{{ill|Lhasa|ja|ラサ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lhasa|1=Lhasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346728}}'s Potala Palace with the Six-Syllable Mantra (left) carved on a rock. On the right is the mantra of {{ill|Vajrasattva|en|Vajrasattva|WD=Q2380086|lt=Vajrasattva}} ({{Unicode|oṃ vajrasattva hūṃ}})]]
'''Six-Syllable Great Bright Mantra''' (六字大明呪, *Rokuji Daimyōju*)<ref name="Iwanami">{{ill|Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja|岩波 仏教辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Buddhist Dictionary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Second Edition, pp. 121-122.</ref> is one of the {{ill|Dharani|en|Dharani|WD=Q1207590|lt=Dhāraṇī}} (mantras) in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}. It is a {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|ja|観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 観音菩薩|1=Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q193849}} dhāraṇī consisting of six {{ill|Syllable|en|Syllable|WD=Q8188|lt=Syllable}}s in {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit}}{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, and because it also consists of six characters in {{ill|Lhasa Tibetan|en|Lhasa Tibetan|WD=Q34271|lt=Tibetan language}}, it is also called the '''Six-Syllable Mantra''' (六字真言, *Rokuji Shingon*){{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|loc={{ill|Yoshiro Imaeda|en|Yoshiro Imaeda|WD=Q6099999|lt=Yutaka Imaeda}} and {{ill|Izumi Hoshi|en|Izumi Hoshi|WD=Q11512946|lt=Hoshii Izumi}}, "Glossary," p. 353}}. It is particularly common in {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|WD=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism}}, where it is frequently recited by Buddhists in {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|WD=Q17252|lt=Tibet}} and {{ill|Mongolia|ja|モンゴル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongolia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モンゴル国|1=Mongolia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q711}}<ref name="Iwanami"/>. Other dhāraṇīs with "six syllables" in their name include the {{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|WD=Q471696|lt=Manjushri}} Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī and the Six-Syllable Phrase Dhāraṇī derived from the *Pranidhi Avalokiteshvara Sutra*{{Efn|Tatsuo Ban’nai, in *Shingon Dharani*, refers to Avalokiteśvara’s Six-Syllable Mantra as the Six-Syllable Dhāraṇī (p. 89).}}.
== Dhāraṇī == This short mantra is '''Om Mani Padme Hum'''{{Efn|Here, the nasal sound represented by {{IAST|ṃ}} or {{Unicode|m̐}} is written as "ン" in Japanese. Variations include "Om Ma Ni Pa Domee Hum"{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=193}}, "Om, Mani, Padmee, Hum"{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}, and "Om Mani Padme Hum"{{Sfn|Tachikawa|1997|p=30}}.}} ({{Lang-sa-short|ॐ मणिपद्मे हूँ}}, {{IAST|oṃ maṇipadme hūṃ}}), composed of six {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|WD=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script}} characters{{Efn|Strictly speaking, "six" here refers to the number of syllables ({{IAST|akṣara}}), not the number of letters{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}.}}.
In Tibetan Buddhism, it is pronounced '''Om Mani Peme Hum'''{{Efn|The romanization of Tibetan varies by scholar. Here, we follow {{Harvnb|Imaeda Supervision|2015}} and {{Harvnb|Tanaka|2009}}. Other versions include "Om Mani Peme Hum" (by Tibetan studies scholar {{ill|Hiromiko Ishihama|ja|石濱裕美子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromiko Ishihama|1=Yumiko Ishihama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4306885}}{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|loc=Ishihama, "People Blessed by Avalokiteśvara," p. 14}}), "Om Mani Peme Hum" (used by early 20th-century Tibetan monk {{ill|Tōkan Tada|ja|多田等観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkan Tada|1=Tōkan Tada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3513681}}<ref>Tada Tōkan, ed. {{ill|Fumiko Makino|ja|牧野文子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumiko Makino|1=Makino Fumiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55560349}}, *Tibetan Stay Diary*, Kodansha, 2009, p. 178.</ref>), and "Om Mani Peme Hum"<ref name="Kawachen">Kelsang Tawwa, ed., *Tibetan-Japanese & Japanese-Tibetan Dictionary*, Kawachen, 2003, p. 218.</ref>.}} ({{Bo|t=ཨོཾ་མ་ཎི་པདྨེ་ཧཱུྃ}}, translated as '''唵嘛呢叭咪吽''' in Chinese).
=== Meaning === "Mani" means "jewel," and "Padme" means "lotus." The dhāraṇī has various interpretations. According to {{ill|Takeshi Itō|ja|伊藤武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshi Itō|1=Takeshi Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380230}}, "jewel" represents the male principle of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=Upaya}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, "lotus" represents the female principle of {{ill|Prajñā (Buddhism)|en|Prajñā (Buddhism)|WD=Q849649|lt=Prajñā}}{{Sfn|Itō|2011|pp=236-237}}, and "Hum" signifies the completion of the mantra. In the {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Vajrayana}} tradition, this dhāraṇī symbolizes enlightenment realized through the union of male and female deities{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=23}}, with the jewel representing the {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|WD=Q695709|lt=Vajra}}, an esoteric symbol of the male organ{{Sfn|Lawson|1994|p=50}}.
== Tibetan Buddhism == The Six-Syllable Mantra is widely recited in Tibet{{Sfn|Mori|2011|p=188}}. It is called *Yige Drukpa* (ཡི་གེ་དྲུག་པ་), meaning "Six-Syllable Mantra"{{Efn|The term *yige* can also mean "syllable," similar to {{IAST|akṣara}}.}}.
Tibet has a long tradition of considering itself blessed by Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Ishihama|2004|p=14}}, and its rulers, such as {{ill|Songtsen Gampo|en|Songtsen Gampo|WD=Q320122|lt=Songtsen Gampo}} and the {{ill|Dalai Lama|en|Dalai Lama|WD=Q37349|lt=Dalai Lamas}}, are regarded as manifestations of Avalokiteśvara{{Sfn|Imaeda Supervision|2015|p=356}}. Thus, the mantra is deeply embedded in Tibetan religious culture. It appears on {{ill|Mani stones|ja|マニ石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mani stones|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Prayer wheel|en|Prayer wheel|WD=Q80876|lt=Prayer wheels}}.
The mantra was transmitted to Tibet as early as the {{ill|Tibetan Empire|en|Tibetan Empire|WD=Q2431480|lt=Tubo}} period, with records of its Tibetan transcription found in the {{ill|Dunhuang manuscripts|en|Dunhuang manuscripts|WD=Q276864|lt=Dunhuang manuscripts}}. The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra*, its earliest textual source, is also listed in the 9th-century *Denkarma Catalog* of Buddhist scriptures{{Sfn|Sakuma|2015|p=200}}.
=== Explanation by the 14th Dalai Lama === The {{ill|14th Dalai Lama|en|14th Dalai Lama|WD=Q17293|lt=14th Dalai Lama}} explains that each syllable of *Om Mani Peme Hum* represents a path to purify impure body, speech, and mind, ultimately leading to Buddhahood{{Refnest|name="tibethouse"|[http://www.tibethouse.jp/about/culture/ommanipadmehum/ Dalai Lama's Office - Tibet Culture - Om Mani Padme Hum]}}.
== Chinese Buddhism == The *Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra* was translated into Chinese by the Indian monk {{ill|Tian Xizai|ja|天息災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tian Xizai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Northern Song dynasty|ja|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} as the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra* (仏説大乗荘厳宝王経), where the mantra is rendered as "唵嘛呢叭咪吽" (Om Mani Padme Hum). This scripture did not gain much traction in China or Japan{{Sfn|Tanaka|2009|pp=46-47}}.
== Effects == According to the *Buddha’s Teaching on the Great Vehicle Adornment Jewel King Sutra*, reciting this dhāraṇī grants protection from disasters, illnesses, and thieves through Avalokiteśvara’s blessings. == See Also == * {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|WD=Q131510|lt=Mantra}} * {{ill|Journey to the West|en|Journey to the West|WD=Q70784|lt=Journey to the West}} - The mantra is used by {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}} to seal {{ill|Sun Wukong|en|Sun Wukong|WD=Q11773777|lt=Sun Wukong}} under the {{ill|Five Elements Mountain|ja|五行山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements Mountain|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== External Links == {{Commons|Om mani padme hum}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OVd2bo9fGSc Tibetan-themed song based on Om Mani Padme Hum]
